title: House prices on road to nowhere, or ‘modest’ squeeze
full_text:
Australia’s house price run is over, with Macquarie analysts warning the market is set for a “modest” correction, or possibly 20 years of prices going nowhere, as the impact of the Albanese government’s tax changes become clear.
Prices are expected to decline by 5 per cent top-to-bottom in one scenario mooted by Macquarie analysts.
The investment analysts at banking major Macquarie called time on house price growth in notes to clients on Wednesday, warning there was also now a distinct possibility Australia could see an unwinding of house prices similar to that seen in the 1980s when prices fell some 9 per cent.
They said the changes to negative gearing and capital gains discounts, as well as the grandfathering of the changes for existing investors, were likely to lead to a slump in new sales which would result in a shortage of new stock and a floor to any price falls.
Macquarie said this could reflect an unwinding of a rise in prices, in a reverse of the rapid run of house prices seen in Australia in 2000 to 2003, after the Howard government introduced the 50 per cent capital gains discount.
However, other analysts at the investment house said it was also quite likely prices would glide sideways over the coming two decades, with investors steering clear from key chunks of the market.
Macquarie said real house prices are now likely to remain at current record levels “for an extended period”.
“We therefore expect its reversal (along with the abolition of negative gearing) to add to the downward pressure already in play,” Macquarie said.
“The irony is of course that if real prices once again move sideways for a decade or two (an outcome needed to “fix” housing affordability), incremental revenue from capital gains tax will be zero: enjoy the tax cut.”
Macquarie noted a key issue was affordability, cautioning house price growth had already been slowing as Australians maxed out their borrowing limits.
The investment house said turnover of property would be the primary impact from the government’s tax changes, warning any shift in the figure would require an economic upset.
This comes amid expectations investors will camp on existing grandfathered properties.
“In the near-term, we expect investor flows into established housing to slow materially – potentially by >50%. While there may be some offset from increased flows into new housing, this will likely be limited by the capacity of the construction sector to deliver this stock,” Macquarie said.
“Stylistically we expect this downturn to be similar to the 2022/23 period, where large falls in listings / stock on the market eventually saw prices stabilise.”
All this is expected to see bank borrowing slow, with Macquarie pointing to an expected contraction in credit growth from 7 per cent in the 2026 financial year to some 3.6 per cent in 2027.
The investment analysts said the lending slump would be worse than expected, having already dialled in a 2 per cent decline estimate before the budget.
“Prior to the tax changes announced in the budget the housing market was already showing signs of slowing, weighed on by RBA rate hikes and an uncertain macro backdrop,” Macquarie analysts said.
Australia’s banks are expected to respond to the slump in new home lending by competing harder for the remaining borrowers in the market.
Macquarie said Commonwealth Bank and Westpac were most exposed to the tax changes, while NAB and ANZ would do better due to their business focus.
“Importantly, these tax changes will be relatively more impactful today, because ~40% of new lending flows are from investors (the highest since 2016), with ~80% of those for established housing,” Macquarie said.
“In the near-term, we expect investor flows into established housing to slow materially – potentially by >50%. While there may be some offset from increased flows into new housing, this will likely be limited by the capacity of the construction sector to deliver this stock.”
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
